title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: begin generating synthetic functional data containing missing values. fit FQPCA model median (q=0.5q = 0.5) 2 components:
code: set.seed(5)  n.obs <- 150 n.time <- 144 Y.axis <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)  # Define PC functions pc1 <- sin(Y.axis) pc2 <- cos(Y.axis)  # Generate individual score coefficients c1.vals <- rnorm(n.obs, mean=0, sd=1) c2.vals <- rnorm(n.obs, mean=0, sd=0.8)  # Construct functional data matrix Y <- c1.vals %*% t(pc1) + c2.vals %*% t(pc2)  # Add noise and some random missing observations (20%) Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(n.obs * n.time, mean=0, sd=0.4), nrow = n.obs) Y[sample(n.obs * n.time, as.integer(0.2 * n.obs * n.time))] <- NA # Fit FQPCA model model_fqpca <- fqpca(   data = Y,   npc = 2,   quantile.value = 0.5,   periodic = FALSE,   splines.df = 10,   seed = 5,   verbose = FALSE )  # Access model components intercept <- model_fqpca$intercept loadings <- model_fqpca$loadings scores <- model_fqpca$scores

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: reconstruct curves compute fitted values, use S3 method fitted(): predict score coordinates new unseen curves, use predict():
code: # Reconstruct training curves using all components Y.fitted <- fitted(model_fqpca)  # Reconstruct curves to achieve 95% proportion of variance explained (PVE) Y.fitted.95 <- fitted(model_fqpca, pve = 0.95) # Generate new data Y.new <- Y[1:10, ]  # Predict scores for new data new_scores <- predict(model_fqpca, newdata = Y.new)

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: package provides two methods control smoothness estimated loading functions: Spline Degrees Freedom: Set directly using splines.df argument. Second Derivative Penalty (Experimental): Set penalized = TRUE supply positive lambda.ridge value. solved using conquer package.
code: # Fit using a second-derivative ridge penalty model_penalized <- fqpca(   data = Y,   npc = 2,   quantile.value = 0.5,   penalized = TRUE,   lambda.ridge = 1e-4,   splines.method = "conquer",   periodic = FALSE,   seed = 5 )

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: can compute percentage explained variability (PVE) component. Visualizing loading functions using package’s generic plot method:
code: # View PVE per component model_fqpca$pve #> [1] 0.6233829 0.3766171  # View cumulative PVE cumsum(model_fqpca$pve) #> [1] 0.6233829 1.0000000 # Plot loadings for components explaining up to 99% variance plot(model_fqpca, pve = 0.99) +    theme_minimal() +    ggtitle("FQPCA Loading Functions")

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: can tune degrees freedom (splines.df) penalty hyperparameter (lambda.ridge) using k-fold cross-validation.

what: Cross-Validation of Splines Degrees of Freedom
title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: can evaluate grid splines.df values measure prediction error using quantile_error():
code: df_grid <- c(5, 8, 12)  # Run CV for df cv_df <- fqpca_cv_df(   data = Y,   splines.df.grid = df_grid,   n.folds = 3,   criteria = "points",   periodic = FALSE,   seed = 5,   verbose.cv = FALSE )  # Mean error matrix cv_df$error.matrix #>         Fold 1    Fold 2    Fold 3 #> [1,] 0.1666141 0.1656331 0.1665625 #> [2,] 0.1645947 0.1633414 0.1639917 #> [3,] 0.1647238 0.1634190 0.1640008  # Find the best df optimal_df <- df_grid[which.min(rowMeans(cv_df$error.matrix))] message("Optimal Degrees of Freedom: ", optimal_df) #> Optimal Degrees of Freedom: 8

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: Similarly, using penalized splines, can evaluate grid ridge penalty parameters:
code: lambda_grid <- c(0, 1e-5, 1e-3)  # Run CV for lambda cv_lambda <- fqpca_cv_lambda(   data = Y,   lambda.grid = lambda_grid,   n.folds = 3,   criteria = "points",   periodic = FALSE,   seed = 5,   verbose.cv = FALSE )  # Error matrix cv_lambda$error.matrix #>         Fold 1    Fold 2    Fold 3 #> [1,] 0.1646853 0.1633307 0.1639331 #> [2,] 0.1646761 0.1633252 0.1639382 #> [3,] 0.1646907 0.1633282 0.1639317  # Find the best lambda optimal_lambda <- lambda_grid[which.min(rowMeans(cv_lambda$error.matrix))] message("Optimal Lambda: ", optimal_lambda) #> Optimal Lambda: 1e-05

title: Getting Started with FQPCA
text: Álvaro Méndez-Civieta, Ying Wei, Keith M Diaz, Jeff Goldsmith. (2025). Functional quantile principal component analysis. Biostatistics, 26(1), kxae040. DOI: 10.1093/biostatistics/kxae040

title: Multilevel Analysis with MFQPCA
text: generate repeated-measures dataset N=200N = 200 curves collected =20I = 20 individuals (10 repeated measurements per individual).
code: set.seed(5)  n.individuals <- 20 n.repeated <- 10 n.time <- 144 N <- n.repeated * n.individuals  group <- rep(seq_len(n.individuals), each = n.repeated) Y.axis <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)  # Define between-subject and within-subject PCs pc.between <- sin(Y.axis) pc.within <- cos(Y.axis)  # Generate scores scores.between <- rnorm(n.individuals) scores.between.full <- scores.between[match(group, unique(group))] scores.within <- rnorm(N)  # Construct data Y <- scores.between.full %*% t(pc.between) + scores.within %*% t(pc.within)  # Add noise and some random missing observations Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(N * n.time, mean=0, sd=0.3), nrow = N) Y[sample(N * n.time, as.integer(0.2 * N * n.time))] <- NA

title: Multilevel Analysis with MFQPCA
text: apply mfqpca() median (q=0.5q = 0.5) 1 -subject component 1 within-subject component:
code: # Fit Multilevel FQPCA model_mfqpca <- mfqpca(   data = Y,   group = group,   npc.between = 1,   npc.within = 1,   quantile.value = 0.5,   periodic = FALSE,   splines.df = 8,   seed = 5,   verbose = FALSE )  # Access components intercept <- model_mfqpca$intercept loadings_between <- model_mfqpca$loadings.between loadings_within <- model_mfqpca$loadings.within scores_between <- model_mfqpca$scores.between scores_within <- model_mfqpca$scores.within

title: Multilevel Analysis with MFQPCA
text: reconstruct curves using multilevel components, use generic fitted() method: predict score components new subjects, use predict(). predicting new subjects, need supply new group membership functional data:
code: # Reconstruct all curves Y.fitted <- fitted(model_mfqpca) # Create dummy new subject with 5 repeated measurements Y.new <- Y[1:5, ] new_group <- rep("new_subject_1", 5)  # Predict multilevel scores new_scores <- predict(   model_mfqpca,   newdata = Y.new,   newdata.group = new_group )  # New predicted scores new_scores$scores.between #>           [,1] #> [1,] -4.838801 head(new_scores$scores.within) #>           [,1] #> [1,]  6.751913 #> [2,]  6.698069 #> [3,] 11.309779 #> [4,]  5.003713 #> [5,]  6.128155

title: Multilevel Analysis with MFQPCA
text: can visualize -subject within-subject loading functions using generic plot() method:
code: # Plot loading functions plot(model_mfqpca) +    theme_minimal() +    ggtitle("Multilevel FQPCA Loadings")

title: Multilevel Analysis with MFQPCA
text: can optimize degrees freedom parameter (splines.df) using k-fold cross-validation specifically adapted multilevel structures.
code: df_grid <- c(5, 8, 10)  # Run CV for multilevel degrees of freedom cv_mfqpca <- mfqpca_cv_df(   data = Y,   group = group,   splines.df.grid = df_grid,   npc.between = 1,   npc.within = 1,   n.folds = 2,   criteria = "points",   periodic = FALSE,   seed = 5,   verbose.cv = FALSE )  # Error matrix cv_mfqpca$error.matrix #>         Fold 1    Fold 2 #> [1,] 0.1259078 0.1264731 #> [2,] 0.1236930 0.1238107 #> [3,] 0.1239126 0.1240724  # Find the best df optimal_df <- df_grid[which.min(rowMeans(cv_mfqpca$error.matrix))] message("Optimal Multilevel Degrees of Freedom: ", optimal_df) #> Optimal Multilevel Degrees of Freedom: 8

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: FunQ R package providing methodologies functional data analysis quantile perspective. implements robust Functional Quantile Principal Component Analysis (FQPCA) Multilevel FQPCA (MFQPCA). Unlike mean-based Functional PCA, FunQ allows model functional curves specific quantile levels (e.g. median extreme quantiles). particularly useful functional curves exhibit skewness, subject-specific heteroscedasticity, outliers.

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: complete, interactive documentation website available : 👉 https://alvaromc317.github.io/FunQ/ website features: Getting Started FQPCA: comprehensive guide single-level functional quantile decomposition, parameter selection, plotting. Multilevel Analysis MFQPCA: detailed tutorial handling repeated-measures hierarchical functional data. Function Reference Index: Detailed descriptions arguments exported functions.

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: can install development version FunQ GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("alvaromc317/FunQ")

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: quick demonstration applying fqpca median (q=0.5q = 0.5) synthetic functional data. First, load libraries:
code: library(FunQ) library(ggplot2)

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: generate synthetic dataset 150 curves 144 time points 20% missing observations:
code: set.seed(5)  n.obs <- 150 n.time <- 144 Y.axis <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)  # Principal component loadings pc1 <- sin(Y.axis) pc2 <- cos(Y.axis)  # Subject-specific scores c1.vals <- rnorm(n.obs, mean=0, sd=1) c2.vals <- rnorm(n.obs, mean=0, sd=0.8)  # Construct curves and add noise + missing values Y <- c1.vals %*% t(pc1) + c2.vals %*% t(pc2) + matrix(rnorm(n.obs * n.time, mean=0, sd=0.4), nrow = n.obs) Y[sample(n.obs * n.time, as.integer(0.2 * n.obs * n.time))] <- NA

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: Fit Functional Quantile PCA model:
code: results <- fqpca(   data = Y,   npc = 2,   quantile.value = 0.5,   periodic = FALSE,   splines.df = 10,   seed = 5 )

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: Use package’s generic plot method visualize estimated loading functions:
code: plot(results, pve = 0.95) +   theme_minimal() +   ggtitle("FQPCA Loading Functions at Median (q = 0.5)")

title: Functional Quantile methodologies
text: Use fqpca_cv_df optimize degrees freedom parameter:
code: df_grid <- c(5, 10, 15) cv_result <- fqpca_cv_df(   data = Y,   splines.df.grid = df_grid,   n.folds = 3,   criteria = "points",   periodic = FALSE,   seed = 5,   verbose.cv = FALSE )  # Mean error per grid value across folds rowMeans(cv_result$error.matrix) #> [1] 0.1662699 0.1640000 0.1639919

title: Fit Yhat — fitted.fqpca_object
code: n.obs = 150 n.time = 144  # Generate scores c1.vals = rnorm(n.obs) c2.vals = rnorm(n.obs)  # Generate pc's pc1 = sin(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)) pc2 = cos(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time))  # Generate data Y <- c1.vals * matrix(pc1, nrow = n.obs, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE) + c2.vals * matrix(pc2, nrow = n.obs, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE)  # Add noise Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(n.obs * n.time, 0, 0.4), nrow = n.obs)  # Add missing observations Y[sample(n.obs*n.time, as.integer(0.2*n.obs*n.time))] <- NA  results <- fqpca(data = Y, npc = 2, quantile.value = 0.5, seed=1)  Yhat <- fitted(object = results, pve=0.99)

title: Fit Yhat — fitted.mfqpca_object
code: n.individuals <- 20 n.repeated <- 10 n.time = 144 N <- n.repeated * n.individuals  group <- rep(1:n.individuals, each=n.repeated)  # Define score values using a normal distribution c1.vals <- rnorm(n.individuals) c1.vals <- c1.vals[match(group, unique(group))] c2.vals <- rnorm(N)  # Define principal components pcb <- sin(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)) pcw <- cos(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time))  # Generate a data matrix and add missing observations Y <- c1.vals * matrix(pcb, nrow = N, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE) + c2.vals * matrix(pcw, nrow = N, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE) Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(N*n.time, 0, 0.4), nrow = N) Y[sample(N*n.time, as.integer(0.2*N))] <- NA  results <- mfqpca(data = Y, group=group, npc.between = 1, npc.within=1, quantile.value = 0.5) Yhat <- fitted(object = results, pve.between=0.95, pve.within=0)

code: n.individuals <- 20 n.repeated <- 10 n.time = 144 N <- n.repeated * n.individuals  group <- rep(1:n.individuals, each=n.repeated)  # Define score values using a normal distribution c1.vals <- rnorm(n.individuals) c1.vals <- c1.vals[match(group, unique(group))] c2.vals <- rnorm(N)  # Define principal components pcb <- sin(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time)) pcw <- cos(seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = n.time))  # Generate a data matrix and add missing observations Y <- c1.vals * matrix(pcb, nrow = N, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE) + c2.vals * matrix(pcw, nrow = N, ncol=n.time, byrow = TRUE)  # Add noise Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(N*n.time, 0, 0.4), nrow = N) Y[sample(N*n.time, as.integer(0.2*N))] <- NA  results <- mfqpca(data = Y, group = group, npc.between = 1, npc.within=1, quantile.value = 0.5)
